items.correct: Experimentation with form (fragmentation, stream of consciousness), focus on subjective interior experience, scepticism toward traditional narrative and absolute truth.
items.misconception: Modernism just means literature written in modern times.
items.random_wrong: Modernism is literature about modern technology like phones.
items.why: Modernism is a specific early 20th-century movement (Joyce, Woolf, Eliot), not a synonym for contemporary. Students use it as a vague chronological term, missing its formal experimentation.

items.correct: A fatal error or flaw in the tragic hero that leads to their downfall — sometimes a character defect, sometimes a mistake in judgement.
items.misconception: Hamartia is the tragic hero's evil personality.
items.random_wrong: Hamartia is the moment the tragic hero dies.
items.why: Tragic heroes are not villains. Hamartia is often a noble quality taken too far (Othello's love, Macbeth's ambition) or a misjudgement — not simple wickedness.

items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.

items.q: Who coined the term unreliable narrator in 1961?
items.why: It's Wayne Booth. “Northrop Frye” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which Marquez novel exemplifies magic realism?
items.misconception: Love in the Time of Cholera
items.why: It's One Hundred Years of Solitude. “Love in the Time of Cholera” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Marquez's One Hundred Years of Solitude published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1967 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
